reflection.quote: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
reflection.reading:
Monday in late June. The kind of day that used to feel like a sentence — alarm, coffee, the long stretch of hours between you and whatever you were going to numb yourself with later. When I was drinking, Mondays were the worst because they were undeniably real. No party to point at, no weekend to hide inside. Just me and my life, staring at each other.

The quiet revolution of sobriety isn't the big stuff. It's not the trips or the summits or the friendships that actually go somewhere. Those are downstream. The revolution is that a Monday in June can just be a Monday. You wake up, your head is clear, you have your own back. You go do the thing in front of you. You're not white-knuckling, you're not performing, you're not waiting for the day to end. You're in it.

Durant's line about excellence as habit gets quoted to death, but it lands differently when you've spent years building the opposite. Drinking was a habit too. So was the lying, the dodging, the constant low-grade self-betrayal. None of that gets unwound in a weekend. It gets unwound on Mondays — ordinary, unremarkable Mondays where you do the next right thing and nobody claps. That's the work. That's also the gift.
reflection.meditation: What ordinary thing can I show up fully for today, without needing it to be more than it is?

affirmations: I am building a life on ordinary days, not just the big ones.
Today I have the clarity to choose my response instead of react.
I am someone I can live with, and that work continues today.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a clear head on a Monday morning — something 22-year-old me couldn't have imagined wanting, let alone having. Grateful for the unremarkable parts of this day: coffee that tastes like coffee, a body that isn't fighting me, a phone I'm not afraid to look at. The small stuff turned out to be the whole thing.
gratitude.closing: One ordinary day at a time. That's the whole practice.
